a
M
   Hot and dense quantum chromodynamics (QCD) matter,
0
known as the quark-gluon plasma (QGP), has been exten-
2
  sively studied in high-energy collisions at  the Relativistic
 
] Heavy-Ion Collider (RHIC) and the CERNLarge Hadron Col-
h  lider (LHC) [1, 2]. Physicists found that at sufficiently high
t
- energies, the QGP exhibits nearly perfect fluid behavior [3–
l
c 7]. In non-central heavy-ion collisions, the huge initial angu-
u  lar momentum renders the QGP the most vortical fluid ever
n observed [8–10]. Furthermore, an extremely strong magnetic
[
  field is generated due to the relativistic motion of the colliding
 
nucleons beams [11–14]. These unique phenomena provide
1
v new insights into the properties of QCD matter.
7  Anisotropic flows, or collective flows, in the final-state par-
3
ticle momentum distribution are among the most fascinating
6
phenomena in heavy-ion collisions, offering unique insights
4
into the dynamics and properties of this strongly-coupled mat-
1
.  ter [5, 15–17]. These flows include the directed flow (v ), el-
1
5
liptic flow (v2), triangular flow (v3), and higher-order harmon-
0
ics [18, 19]. Significant anisotropic flows have been observed
5
2 not only in non-central heavy-ion collisions but also in small
: systems such as p+Au, d+Au, He+Au at RHIC [20, 21], and
v
i p+Pb collisions at the LHC [22–24]. Hydrodynamic models
reveal that these flows are highly sensitive to the initial condi-
X
r  tions of the QGP evolution [25–33].  Important insights have
a
been gained into the impact of initial geometry, overlap fluc-
tuations, and spatial anisotropies on the final-state anisotropic
flow, driven by pressure gradients in hydrodynamic mod-
els [34–36] or particle interactions in AMPT transport mod-
els [37, 38].

ntation region profile. By analyzing the ∆v2 and ∆v2/v2
certainties of the QGP initial state and provide additional
Recently,  the splitting of elliptic flow (∆v2) on opposite
sides of the impact parameter axis has emerged as a promising
observable for constraining the initial state of the QGP [39–
41]. Studies within both transport and hydrodynamic frame-
works suggest that ∆v2 is closely related to the non-zero an-
gular momentum or the longitudinal tilted geometry of the
initial QGP fireball, with the directed flow (v1) being the pri-
mary contributor [40, 41].  In this study, we revisit ∆v2(η)
√
and v2(pT) in non-central Au+Au collisions at  sNN = 200
GeV. Using the state-of-the-art three-dimensional initial con-
dition model TRENTo-3D [42] to generate the initial energy
density and coupling it with the (3+1)-dimensional CCNU-
LBNL-Viscous hydrodynamic (CLVisc) model [34, 35], we
explore the splitting of elliptic flow. Our results reveal that
∆v2 is primarily driven by odd flow harmonics, with v1 being
the dominant contributor to ∆v2(η). However, unlike previ-
ous studies, we find that triangular flow (v3) plays a signif-
icant role in ∆v2(pT), particularly in the pseudo-rapidity re-
gion |η| < 1.3.
To further  investigate the sensitivity of ∆v2  to the ini-
tial state, we systematically examine the influence of sub-
nucleonic degrees of freedom (nc, or “hotspots”), transverse
momentum scale (kT), and fragmentation profile parameters
(α and β). We find that ∆v2(pT) is sensitive to all three ef-
fects in the TRENTo-3D initialization, while ∆v2(η) exhibits
strong sensitivity to kT, which generates a tilted geometry of
the QGP fireball, but minimal sensitivity to nc, α, and β.
These findings suggest that ∆v2 could be a valuable observ-
able for probing the initial state of the QGP fireball and refin-
ing parameters in the TRENTo-3D model. Additionally, we
present predictions for ∆v2/v2 ratio as a function of η and pT.
We find that ∆v2 can complement v1 and v3 in constraining
the three-dimensional properties of the QGP fireball created
in heavy-ion collisions.
The article is organized as follows.  In Sec. II, we present
the theoretical framework for investigating the splitting of
elliptic flow,  including the TRENTo-3D initialization with
a tilted medium geometry,  the (3+1)-dimensional hydrody-

√

condition for the hydrodynamic evolution of the QGPmedium

TRENTo’s participant thickness formalism, the three dimen-
sional energy density profile at the initial proper time is

The  longitudinal  structure of  the QGP fireball  in  the
TRENTo-3D initialization is characterized by both central and
fragmentation regions, governed by the following key param-
eters [42]:

In this work, we focus on the influence of subnucleonic de-
grees of freedom (nc), the transverse momentum scale (kT),
and the fragmentation profile parameters (α and β) on the el-
liptic flow splitting (∆v2) of charged particles.  The results
and detailed analysis are presented in Section III. The energy
deposition in the model dynamically adapts according to:

which captures the transition from strong net-baryon accumu-
lation at RHIC energies to midrapidity-dominated deposition
at LHC energies. The TRENTo-3D initialization parameters
used in this study are based on Ref. [42], which have been val-
idated through Bayesian calibration to dNch/dη distributions
across the energy range of 200 GeV–5.02 TeV. Those parame-
ters successfully reproduces the dNch/dη distributions in both
p+A and A+A collisions.
B.  CLVisc hydrodynamic simulations
Starting with the TRENTo-3D initial condition constructed
in the previous subsection, we utilize a (3+1)-D viscous hy-
s)  drodynamic model CLVisc to describe the evolution of the
,
QGP medium and further particlization. The CLVisc model is
described in full detail in Refs. [34, 35]. We repeat here only

We implement the HotQCD2014 equation of state (EOS)
to solve the hydrodynamic equations  [44, 45]. The freeze-

3
0.4 GeV/fm , where we construct the freeze-out hypersurface
and perform hadron sampling using the Cooper-Frye formal-
ism [34, 35]. The resonance decay contributions also included
in the simulation. It is worth noting that the present framework
does not account for hadronic rescattering effects during the
later-stage evolution of the system.
Utilizing the aforementioned parameters, we present  in
Fig. 1 the energy density distributions density in the ηs-x plane
for a single event (somth, averaged over 2000 events) in 5-
√
40% centrality Au+Au collisions at  sNN = 200 GeV, evalu-
ated at proper time τ = 1, 3, 6 fm (from left to right panel).
The figure reveals that the QGP fireball manifests a distinct
tilted and asymmetric geometry along the longitudinal direc-
tion during its evolution under the TRENTo-3D initialization
+ CLVisc hydrodynamic framework. This geometric distor-
tion is anticipated to be more pronounced in peripheral colli-
sions, owing to enhanced drag forces exerted by spectator nu-
cleons on participants. Such energy density asymmetry plays
a crucial role in generating rapidity-odd directed flow of soft

C.  Left-right splitting of the elliptic flow
The normalized azimuthal distribution of hadrons in mo-
mentum space, characterized by a given transverse momen-
tum pT and either rapidity y or pseudorapidity η, can be ex-
pressed as [18, 40]

A novel approach has been recently proposed to quantify
the right (shorted as “R”)-left (shorted as “L”) elliptic flow

In this section, we present our calculation and prediction for
∆v2 with respect to the reaction plane, using the TRENTo-3D
initial condition and CLVisc hydrodynamic simulation. These
results are compared with pre-existing experimental data.

In Fig. 2, we present the directed flow (v1) of charged parti-
cles as a function of pseudo-rapidity (upper panel) and trans-
verse momentum (lower panel) in 5-40% Au+Au collisions
√
at  sNN=200 GeV. The calculations are carried out using the
TRENTo-3D smooth initial condition (averaged 2000 events)
and (3+1)-D CLVisc hydrodynamic simulations. We find that
TRENTo-3D + CLVisc framework provide a reasonable de-
scription for the STAR experimental data. This suggests that
the directed flow is influenced by the tilted geometry of the
QGP fireball, aligning with previous research [40, 41]. Thus,
we expect TRENTo-3D + CLVisc framework to provide cor-
rect prediction and deep understanding of ∆v2.
In the upper panel of Fig. 3, we display the model predic-
tion for elliptic flow splitting (∆v2) of charged particles as a
function of pseudorapidity (η) in mid-central (5-40%) Au+Au
√
collisions at  sNN = 200 GeV. We additionally plot odd har-
monics with fixed coefficients as presented in Eq. (21), which
constitute the dominant contribution to ∆v2. We find that the
8v1
splitting primarily originates from the term 3π (1−3v2), with
∆v2 showing equivalent sign dependence. The contributions
to ∆v2(η) from v3 and v5 harmonics, though sign-opposed to
v1, remain negligible over the full η range.

on transverse momentum (pT ) at mid-rapidity (|η| < 1.3) in
Au+Au collisions.  It is interesting to see that v3 constitutes
the dominant contribution to ∆v2(pT), while contributions
from v5 harmonics remain negligible. A cancellation effect
emerges at low pT (pT < 1.2 GeV) for ∆v2(pT), due to the
negative v1 and positive v3 yield at low pT. Then enhanced
magnitude of ∆v2(pT) compared to prior studies [40, 41]
arises from a different longitudinally tilted initial geometric
configuration in TRENTo-3D initialization.
We now examine the influence of  the number of con-
stituents (or “hotspots”) per nucleon (nc), transverse momen-
tum scale (kT), and fragmentation profile parameters (α and
β) on the elliptic flow splitting ∆v2.
We first investigate the impact of the number of constituents
(nc) on ∆v2. The upper panel of Fig. 4 displays the ∆v2(η)
√
distributions for Au+Au collisions at  sNN = 200 GeV (5–
40% centrality, 0 < pT < 3.5 GeV) for different nc values.
The smooth initial condition suppresses spatial fluctuations of
sub-nucleonic structures through transverse-plane averaging,
resulting in minimal impact on∆v2(η). In the lower panel, we
present ∆v2 as a function of pT. Enhanced “hotspot” fluctua-
tions at larger nc correlate with decreased ∆v2(pT), suggest-

ing a connection between ∆v2 enhancement and the growth of

ations, as indicated in previous studies [50, 51].
In Fig.

5, we systematically explore the role of the parton
transverse momentum scale (kT), which governs the initial-
state tilt geometry of the quark-gluon plasma (QGP) fireball,
on ∆v2. The upper panel illustrates the η dependence of ∆v2,
where ∆v2(η) exhibits sensitivity to kT for |η| < 1. Larger kT
values enhance ∆v2, attributed to the increased tilt of the QGP
fireball in the reaction plane, which amplifies v1 and conse-
quently ∆v2 (refer to Eq. (21)). Conversely, the lower panel
reveals an inverse relationship between kT and ∆v2(pT), as
larger kT suppresses ∆v2(pT) due to a reduction in triangu-
lar flow (v3). This kT-dependent modulation arises from the
altered initial-state geometry and subsequent hydrodynamic
evolution of the QGP.
In Fig. 6, we investigate the impact of the fragmentation
profile shape parameter α on ∆v2. The parameter α controls
the longitudinal distribution of the QGP fireball in the for-
ward/backward rapidity regions. As shown in the upper panel,
∆v2(η) exhibits minimal sensitivity to α within |η| < 2.5, in-
dicating that ∆v2 in the central rapidity region is governed
by other dynamical factors. The lower panel reveals a non-

monotonic dependence of ∆v2(pT) on α: ∆v2(pT) initially
decreases with α, and then increases for larger α. This be-
havior arises from the dual role of α in modulating triangular
flow (v3), where moderate α suppresses v3(pT), while larger
α enhances it.
In Fig. 7, we examine the effect of the fragmentation pro-
file shape parameter β on ∆v2. Similar to α, β governs the
longitudinal geometry of the QGP fireball. As shown in the
upper panel, ∆v2(η) changes monotonically with increasing
β within |η| < 2.5, as larger β values produce a flatter lon-
gitudinal distribution, reducing initial-state anisotropy.  The
lower panel reveals that samller β values correspond to a re-
duced ∆v2(pT), as a flatter initial-state fragmentation profile
diminishes anisotropic flow development.
From Figs. 4–7, we conclude that ∆v2(pT) is sensitive to
nc, kT, α, and β, making it as a valuable probe for exploring
the initial state of the QGP and for refining and constraining
parameters in the TRENTo-3D initial condition model.
It is well studied that various systematic uncertainties in the
transverse initial condition models can lead to significant vari-
ations in the elliptic flow v2 [52, 53]. These model dependen-
cies can also affect predictions of the elliptic flow difference.

Using the TRENTo-3D initial condition model coupled
with (3+1)-dimensional CLVisc hydrodynamic simulations,
we have revisited the left-right splitting of the elliptic flow

(∆v2) in Au+Au collisions at  sNN = 200 GeV. We find
that ∆v2 is primarily driven by odd flow harmonics, with the
directed flow (v1) being the dominant contributor to ∆v2(η),
consistent with previous transport and hydrodynamic model
studies. However, unlike earlier findings, our results reveal
that triangular flow (v3) plays a significant role in ∆v2(pT),
particularly in the rapidity region |η| < 1.3, where it becomes
the dominant contributor.
To explore the sensitivity of ∆v2 to the initial state, we
systematically investigated the influence of sub-nucleonic de-
grees of freedom (nc, or “hotspots” number), transverse mo-
mentum scale (kT), and fragmentation profile parameters (α
and β). Our analysis shows that ∆v2(pT) is sensitive to all
three effects in the TRENTo-3D initialization.  In contrast,
∆v2(η) exhibits strong sensitivity to kT, which induces a tilted
geometry of the QGP fireball in the initial state, but minimal
sensitivity to nc, α, and β. These findings highlight ∆v2 as
a valuable observable for probing the initial state of the QGP
and constraining parameters in the TRENTo-3D model.
Finally, we present predictions for ∆v2/v2 as a function of
η and pT. We find that the slope d[∆v2/v2]/dη reaches 4.4%
for |η| < 1.0, and ∆v2/v2 cross zero-point at pT ∼ 1.5 GeV.
Our study demonstrates that ∆v2 can complement v1 and v3 in
constraining the three-dimensional profile of the QGP fireball
created in heavy-ion collisions.
